title: Google Tensor Processing Unit (TPU) Specifications: v4, v5e, v5p, v6e, and Trillium for AI Training and Inference
text:
The Google TPU v4, released in 2022, delivers 275 TFLOPS of BF16 performance per chip and 275 TOPS of INT8 throughput per chip. Each TPU v4 chip is equipped with 32GB of HBM2e memory and provides 1.2 TB/s of memory bandwidth. The estimated thermal design power (TDP) is approximately 170W per chip, based on pod-level power measurements. TPU v4 chips communicate via ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect), a custom high-bandwidth topology designed by Google. A full TPU v4 Pod scales up to 4,096 chips, delivering approximately 1.1 exaFLOPS of aggregate BF16 performance. TPU v4 is available exclusively through Google Cloud. The TPU v4 generation was used to train landmark models including PaLM and Gemini 1.0.

The Google TPU v5e, introduced in 2023, is optimized for cost-efficient inference and small-to-mid-size model training. The TPU v5e delivers approximately 197 TFLOPS of BF16 performance per chip, tuned for efficiency rather than peak throughput. INT8 performance is approximately 394 TOPS per chip. Each chip contains 16GB of HBM2e memory with a memory bandwidth of 820 GB/s. The estimated TDP is approximately 100W per chip, making it one of the most power-efficient AI accelerators available. TPU v5e pod slices scale up to 256 chips. Google reports that the TPU v5e achieves 2x better cost-performance compared to TPU v4 for serving workloads. The v5e is designed primarily for mid-size models and inference-heavy deployments.

The Google TPU v5p, also released in 2023, is the high-performance training variant of the fifth-generation TPU family. The TPU v5p delivers 459 TFLOPS of BF16 performance per chip and 918 TOPS of INT8 throughput per chip. Each chip is equipped with 95GB of HBM2e memory, providing 3x more HBM capacity than the TPU v4. Memory bandwidth reaches 2.76 TB/s per chip. The estimated TDP is approximately 250W per chip. TPU v5p pods scale up to 8,960 chips, achieving approximately 4.1 exaFLOPS of aggregate BF16 performance. Google reports 2x training performance improvement over TPU v4. The TPU v5p was used for training Gemini 1.5.

The Google TPU v6e, codenamed Trillium and released in 2024, is Google's latest generation TPU. The estimated BF16 performance is approximately 918 TFLOPS per chip, based on Google's claim of roughly 2x improvement over the v5p per chip. INT8 performance is estimated at approximately 1,836 TOPS per chip. Each chip contains 32GB of HBM memory (HBM2e or HBM3) with an estimated memory bandwidth of approximately 1.6 TB/s. The estimated TDP is approximately 200W per chip. Google reports a 4.7x improvement in compute performance per dollar compared to the TPU v5e. Energy efficiency is improved by 67% over the v5e. TPU v6e pod slices support up to 256 chips. The ICI bandwidth reaches 7.2 Tbps per chip, representing a 3x increase over the v5e.

Google reports that TPUs are 2-5x more energy efficient per FLOP than comparable GPUs for many machine learning workloads. Google's custom-designed datacenters achieve a Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) of 1.1, meaning only 10% of total power consumption goes to cooling and other overhead. TPU pods utilize direct liquid cooling to manage thermal loads efficiently. Google matches 100% of its electricity consumption with renewable energy purchases. Additionally, Google employs carbon-aware computing, shifting TPU workloads to regions with cleaner electrical grids to further reduce the carbon footprint of AI training and inference.

When comparing TPUs to NVIDIA GPUs, the TPU v5p and NVIDIA H100 offer comparable peak performance per chip, but TPU pods scale to thousands of chips using the custom ICI interconnect. For the TPU v6e versus the H100, Google claims 4.7x better price-performance. A key advantage of the TPU platform is its tight integration with the Google Cloud TPU software stack, including JAX and XLA. A key limitation is that TPUs are available only on Google Cloud and have a less flexible software ecosystem than NVIDIA's CUDA platform.
